ABSTRACT
--------

Despite significant advancement in conventional desalination technologies, their widespread application is still constrained by high energy demands, high capital costs, and the associated greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The main objective of this study is to evaluate the potential and challenges of renewable energy desalination in the Middle East. The study also compared the production of energy and water, and emissions reductions of hypothetical 100 MW renewable energy plants in Kuwait, coupled with reverse osmosis (RO) desalination units. The wind-RO plant is estimated to produce 68 million cubic metres on an annual basis, while the CSP-RO and PV-RO plants produced 44 and 37 million cubic metres of fresh water, respectively. These estimates, however, only account for 5% to 9% of the annual fresh water demand in the reference case. Meeting 100% of the fresh water demand would require a 1000 MW to 2000 MW renewable energy capacity. Overall, the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources is a fundamental barrier to the large-scale transition to renewable energies for desalination. The results of this study indicate that the CSP’s relatively small footprint (compared to wind plants) and higher capacity factor make it an ideal compromise among the proposed plants.

KEYWORDS:

*   [Desalination](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/Desalination)
*   [renewable energy](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/renewable+energy)
*   [middle east](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/middle+east)
*   [energy transition](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/energy+transition)

1. Introduction
---------------

The rapid economic growth, growing population, as well as the high incomes and standards of living in the middle east have caused an increase in the domestic energy consumption, which consists primarily of hydrocarbons. Additionally, the hot climate and the limited freshwater resources are increasing the energy demand, due to the need for year-round air condition and water desalination. The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries have the highest percentage of installed desalination capacity globally (47.5%), as well as a predicted ‘extremely high’ water stress by 2040 (Bundschuh et al. [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Currently, almost all the Middle East’s desalination plants are powered by fossil fuels (93% gas, 6% oil, and 1% nuclear) (ROPME [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Most desalination plants in the region are coupled with power plants (i.e. co-generation plants) to utilise the waste thermal energy from the power generation in the thermal desalination process. Consequently, this results in a cost reduction as well as an overall reduction in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Furthermore, by generating both heat and electricity, such co-generation plants enhance the reliability of both the desalination and power generation processes, allowing for improved demand matching between power and water (Pleis and Zhou [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Large scale seawater desalination is an energy-intensive process, and its reliance on fossil fuels has put additional pressures on water-energy infrastructure to meet the growing demands while minimising the carbon footprint. Thus, there has been a drive across the countries in the region to diversify their energy mix and reduce their dependence on fossil fuels, leading to an increased focus on renewable energy sources. Thus, the objective of this study is to assess the potential of renewable energy-driven desalination, emission reduction, and the associated integration challenges. This study’s novelty stems from two main aspects. Firstly, the System Advisor Model (SAM) software package was used to predict and compare the capacity factors, monthly energy production, and energy yield of photovoltaic (PV), concentrated solar power (CSP), and wind energy plants located in Kuwait. Secondly, the study estimated the water production from renewable energy-powered reverse osmosis (RO) plants, as well as the corresponding reduction in GHG emissions.

2. Desalination processes
-------------------------

In brief, the process of desalination involves the treatment of either brackish or seawater into freshwater. The desalination processes can be initially subdivided into two subgroups, specifically the thermal desalination and membrane processes, which can be paired together as a hybrid to maximise the advantages of both systems.

### 2.1. Thermal desalination

The two main thermal desalination processes are multistage flash distillation (MSF) and multiple effect distillation (MED). Both use evaporation and condensation to convert saline water into fresh water, utilising electrical and thermal energies. Thermal desalination, MSF and MED specifically, are the predominating desalination technologies in the GCC region, where large co-generation plants exist with low-cost waste heat, thereby reducing production costs. Additionally, thermal desalination is suitable for the high salinity of seawater and water temperatures in the region (Esmaeilion [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); World Bank [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Thermal plants are reliable, with extended lifespans, and with similar engineering features to the coupled power plants (Borsani and Rebagliati [Citation 2005](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). The main disadvantage of the thermal desalination process is that the recovery ratio is 10–20%, meaning it uses an exorbitant amount of water and more energy than other desalination processes (Abdelkareem et al. [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)).

### 2.2. Membrane desalination

Membrane desalination involves propelling seawater through a semi-permeable membrane to remove salts and minerals. The two main membrane desalination processes are RO and membrane distillation (MD). MD is a hybrid of thermal desalination and membrane desalination; it uses a pressure difference driven by a slight temperature gradient to separate feedwater through a hydrophobic membrane. MD is an attractive cost-effective desalination option; it utilises low-grade waste heat from either power plants or thermal renewable energy sources, such as geothermal and solar (Ghaffour et al. [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Goosen, Mahmoudi, and Ghaffour [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Zaragoza, Ruiz-Aguirre, and Guillén-Burrieza [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). The major constraints for MD are membrane performance, flux stability, thermal efficiency, and temperature polarisation. RO, on the other hand, utilises mechanical or electrical power to drive saltwater through membranes to separate salt ions and other dissolved solids. The most popular method of membrane desalination, and desalination in general, is seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO), which accounts for 80% of the desalination technologies installed worldwide (Aladwani, Al-Obaidi, and Mujtaba [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Overall, RO is the preferred method of desalination because of its low energy requirements and high recovery rates of 30–50%. There are some downsides to SWRO, most notably membrane fouling due to the accumulation of salts and other solids on the surface, leading to a decrease in influx and an increase in the cost of operations (World Bank [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Additionally, SWRO is affected by feedwater quality and temperature, requiring the pre- and post-treatment of water, which can increase production costs (Noreddine, Missimer, and Amy [Citation 2013](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). It has an average recovery ratio of 45% for seawater salinity of 35,000 mg/l, but the seawater salinity is usually higher in Middle Eastern locations, such as the Arabian Gulf and the Red Sea, resulting in significantly lower recovery rates (Noreddine, Missimer, and Amy [Citation 2013](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). The major advantages of RO technology are less energy consumption compared to thermal processes, a small footprint, and the use of non-corrosive equipment all of which have resulted in RO becoming the leading desalination technology globally.

3. Renewable energy integration with desalination plants
--------------------------------------------------------

The most popular type of desalination plant in the Middle East is thermal desalination, since it accommodates the warmer temperature, increased salinity, and higher concentration of organics in the Arabian Gulf while also utilising low-cost power generating turbines using fossil fuels. However, the more cost-effective renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind, mainly benefit RO, which uses electrical power. Selecting the most appropriate renewable energy configuration depends on the desalination plant’s location, access to the grid, availability of the renewable energy source, size of the desalination plant, and salinity of the raw water source. Figure 1 shows the potential for coupling renewable energy technologies with desalination methods. Generally, renewable energy driven desalination systems can be divided into two categories: thermal and electrical processes.

Figure 1.  Possible renewable energy sources integrated with desalination processes.

[![Image 8: The figure shows the potential for coupling renewable energy technologies with desalination methods.](https://www.tandfonline.com/cms/asset/add0f79f-f389-41a7-89f8-72af5b8a3da4/tsue_a_2394552_f0001_oc.jpg)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)

Display full size

### 3.1. Solar energy

The use of solar energy is the most common worldwide and has the highest potential for renewable energy-driven desalination for both thermal and electric-based methods. In the GCC region, solar energy is the fastest-growing technology, accounting for 98% of renewable energy (Figure 2). The two main solar energy technologies are Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) and Photovoltaic (PV) systems.

Figure 2.  2021 renewable energy portfolio in the GCC. Data source (IRENA [Citation 2023a](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)).

Renewable energy portfolio in the GCC. PV accounts for 94% of renewable energy followed by wind at 4%.

[![Image 9: Figure 2. 2021 renewable energy portfolio in the GCC. Data source (IRENA Citation2023a).](https://www.tandfonline.com/cms/asset/dd44abfd-331f-49a9-983a-5dba0059fe9f/tsue_a_2394552_f0002_oc.jpg)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)

Display full size

CSP systems collect and concentrate solar energy, direct normal irradiance (DNI), to produce thermal energy, which is used to generate steam to drive conventional thermal power systems to transform thermal energy into electrical energy (Esmaeilion [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Sayed et al. [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). The main CSP technologies are: parabolic trough collector, central solar tower, linear Fresnel reflector, and parabolic dish (Fernández et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). A study by Baig, Surovtseva, and Halawa ([Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)) compared the performance of multiple solar collectors, and concluded that parabolic trough collectors have superior maturity level and reliability.

CSP technologies can be coupled with existing thermal power plants by utilising only the solar fields to produce thermal power. This configuration has the advantage of generating renewable and dispatchable electrical power while minimising the overall investment cost. CSP systems can also be integrated with thermal desalination units by utilising the low to medium pressure steam exiting the thermal power system (Palenzuela, César Alarcón-Padilla, and Zaragoza [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). However, this integration requires increasing the temperature of the exhaust steam, which requires additional energy or intermediate extraction of steam from the power cycle, leading to decreased electrical power production (Palenzuela, César Alarcón-Padilla, and Zaragoza [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Alternatively, the electrical power from a CSP plant can power a RO plant. The Al Noor CSP 550 MW power plant in Morocco is the world’s largest CSP plant. A study by (Kettani and Bandelier [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)) investigated the use of CSP with storage and the grid (based on Al Noor plant) to drive a 275,000 L/d RO plant. The results showed that desalinated water could be produced at around 1 USD/m 3 (Kettani and Bandelier [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)).

Solar PV directly converts sunlight into electricity using the semiconductor material found in solar cells (Ahmed, Hashaikeh, and Hilal [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Sayed et al. [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Solar PV, paired with RO, is the most common renewable energy used for desalination (Ahmed, Hashaikeh, and Hilal [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Kettani and Bandelier [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Several studies have investigated the economic feasibility of standalone PV-RO systems. These systems can be operated under a wide range of solar radiation (400 to 1200 W/m 2) to produce high quality water; however, the amount produced varies with fluctuations in solar intensity (Richards et al. [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#)). Batteries are commonly used to deal with any intermittent energy issues; however, the high cost and short lifespans of batteries in hot regions are common barriers to their usage (Alghoul et al. [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Darwish et al. [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19397038.2024.2394552#); Mo

enriched_points.expanded_content: Solar and wind energy technologies significantly enhance desalination efficiency, utilizing renewable sources to power reverse osmosis and thermal desalination processes, reducing fossil fuel dependency.
Successful projects, like CSP-RO systems in Morocco and PV-RO setups in Kuwait, demonstrate effective integration of renewable energy in desalination, ensuring sustainable water production.
Utilizing renewable energy for desalination decreases greenhouse gas emissions, lowers operational costs, and enhances energy security, promoting long-term sustainability in water production.
